ALL GIVE ORCHARD THE lie

Adams and Others Named 40 AccompIIcoe


Declare He Testimony False.

pages.raw_text:
I He admitted knowing Jim War
ford, now in the penitentiary, who
was formerly mine owners gun
man at Cripple Creek. Asked II he
knew mine owners, managers, dep
uty sheriffs and militia officers he


said he mex a number of them, but


swore he did not convey any In


formation to any of them regarding
the plan to blow up the Indepen.
dence depot and said he did NOT


know chat a military company was


on guard there. They had been or
dered to be under arms at the mine
that night, hours before the ex.
plosion, because they had been told
something was coming on" u
Orchard did not know where he


met Adams on the day OF the explo-
sion. He denied that he had been
cautioned not to fix either time or
place. He said Hawley and McPar-
land simply told him to go ahead |
and tell the truth. This caused a |
laugh in the courtroom, which was !,
quickly suppressed.
UP to the time of the depot excl
plosion Orchard said he had re-
ceived s735 from Meyer and Hay
wood He denied receiving letters /
from Superintendent Bangs Of tho
Pinkerton agency, or VIM. Pinker-
ton, of Chicago, telling him what


course to pursue and what would


be done for him after the trial.
Richardson devoted nearly three
quarters of his questioning to


money Orchard said he got, but the
witness memory on this point was
haZy as to amounts or dates. Rich


ardson got another laugh from the


crowd by asking why witness used
| the word assassination when speak.
ing Of killing, demanding If 1t came


from association with MCFARLAND.
Orchard said no; he had learned


the meaning of the word since he


had been in the penitentiary.
He declared he didn't plan to ex-
plode the powder at Independence
| before the train arrived, but when
asked IL he did not actually do So
said he didn't want to injure any
railroad men, so he exploded 1t
when he saw the headlight coming.
1t is apparent that Richardson Ia
trying to show the blowing up OF
/ the depot was not intended to kill


any one, but was a plot to blow up


a deserted place and give an excuse


for keeping militia In the district.


Witness insisted he never knew


there were nonunion men in the


depot about the time of the explo-


sion. Afterward he went to Denver


and told Simpkins about 1t.


Haywood and Pettibone came up.


he said, and the matter was dis


cussed. They said 1t was good


job, Orchard testified.


James Hirwin, now acting secres


tary of the union, was also there,


but left before he talked about the
crime.
